quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reaction rate"?

options.text: FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges
One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B (e.g., 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂)
(Mass of desired product / Total mass of all products) × 100%; measures efficiency of atom utilization in a reaction
The change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time: rate = -Δ[A]/Δt; typically in mol/(L·s) or M/s

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reaction rate" is defined as: The change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time: rate = -Δ[A]/Δt; typically in mol/(L·s) or M/s. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Average rate"?

options.text: The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria
Equilibrium constant for weak acid dissociation: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; larger Ka = stronger acid
The change in concentration over a finite time interval: Δ[A]/Δt; decreases over time as reactants are consumed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Average rate" is defined as: The change in concentration over a finite time interval: Δ[A]/Δt; decreases over time as reactants are consumed. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Instantaneous rate"?

options.text: The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve
Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
The arrangement of only the atoms (bonding pairs) around the central atom (e.g., bent for 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Instantaneous rate" is defined as: The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Initial rate"?

options.text: A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)
The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea
The instantaneous rate at t = 0; used to determine rate laws because reverse reactions are negligible at the start
Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer; typically between metals and nonmetals (ΔEN > 1.7)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Initial rate" is defined as: The instantaneous rate at t = 0; used to determine rate laws because reverse reactions are negligible at the start. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Factors affecting rate"?

options.text: Concentration (more collisions), temperature (faster molecules), surface area (more exposed reactant), catalysts (lower Ea)
Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)
Equilibrium involving species in more than one phase; pure solids and liquids have activity = 1 and are excluded from K

quiz_questions.explanation: "Factors affecting rate" is defined as: Concentration (more collisions), temperature (faster molecules), surface area (more exposed reactant), catalysts (lower Ea). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time: rate = -Δ[A]/Δt; typically in mol/(L·s) or M/s"?

options.text: Lewis structure
ΔS prediction rules
Hybridization
Reaction rate

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Reaction rate".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The change in concentration over a finite time interval: Δ[A]/Δt; decreases over time as reactants are consumed"?

options.text: Average rate
Ion-dipole force
Half-reaction method
ΔG° = -nFE°

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Average rate".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve"?

options.text: Green chemistry
Heterogeneous catalyst
Instantaneous rate
Temperature change

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Instantaneous rate".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The instantaneous rate at t = 0; used to determine rate laws because reverse reactions are negligible at the start"?

options.text: pOH
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Decomposition reaction
Initial rate

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Initial rate".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Concentration (more collisions), temperature (faster molecules), surface area (more exposed reactant), catalysts (lower Ea)"?

options.text: Ion-dipole force
Factors affecting rate
Heat of vaporization
Chromatography

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Factors affecting rate".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Reaction Rates is accurate?

options.text: Reaction Rates measure how fast reactant concentrations decrease or product concentrations increase over time.
Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reaction Rates as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Reaction Rates?

options.text: Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.
Rate = -Δ[reactant]/Δt = Δ[product]/Δt (adjusted by stoichiometric coefficients).
Strong acid–strong base titrations have equivalence point at pH 7.
Atomic radius decreases left to right (increasing Zeff) and increases top to bottom (more electron shells).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reaction Rates as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Reaction Rates?

options.text: Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
Rates depend on concentration, temperature, surface area, and catalysts.
Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).
Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reaction Rates as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Reaction Rates?

options.text: The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.
Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
The initial rate method measures rate at t = 0 to avoid complications from reverse reactions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reaction Rates as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Reaction Rates?

options.text: Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
Rates generally decrease over time as reactants are consumed.
Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Reaction Rates as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Reaction Rates?

options.text: Swimming pool chemistry: maintaining pH 7.2-7.8 using acid (HCl) or base (NaHCO₃) additions to optimize chlorine disinfection effectiveness
Food preservation through refrigeration: lowering temperature slows the rate of bacterial growth and enzymatic decomposition reactions
pH meters measuring voltage differences between a glass electrode and reference electrode, converted to pH via the Nernst equation
Fluorine's extreme electronegativity making it the most reactive nonmetal, used in Teflon (PTFE) coatings and uranium enrichment (UF₆)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reaction Rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Reaction Rates?

options.text: Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Catalytic converters in cars: platinum/palladium catalysts speed up the conversion of toxic CO, NOₓ, and unburned hydrocarbons to CO₂, N₂, and H₂O
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reaction Rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Reaction Rates applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Antacid tablet surface area experiments: crushed tablets dissolve faster than whole tablets because increased surface area increases reaction rate
Ocean buffering against acidification: carbonate/bicarbonate system absorbing excess CO₂, but capacity being overwhelmed by current emission rates
Kidney stone formation: when [Ca²⁺][C₂O₄²⁻] exceeds Ksp of calcium oxalate (2.3 × 10⁻⁹), crystals precipitate in the kidney
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reaction Rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Reaction Rates?

options.text: Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Food preservation through refrigeration: lowering temperature slows the rate of bacterial growth and enzymatic decomposition reactions
Catalytic converters using Pt, Pd, and Rh heterogeneous catalysts to lower Ea for converting exhaust pollutants (CO, NOₓ) to harmless products
SN1 and SN2 mechanisms in organic chemistry: SN1 (two-step, carbocation intermediate) vs. SN2 (one-step, bimolecular) determine reaction outcomes in drug synthesis

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reaction Rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Reaction Rates in practice?

options.text: Swimming pool chemistry: maintaining pH 7.2-7.8 using acid (HCl) or base (NaHCO₃) additions to optimize chlorine disinfection effectiveness
Radioactive decay following first-order kinetics with constant half-life: carbon-14 (t₁/₂ = 5,730 years) used for archaeological dating
Catalytic converters in cars: platinum/palladium catalysts speed up the conversion of toxic CO, NOₓ, and unburned hydrocarbons to CO₂, N₂, and H₂O
Road salt (NaCl) lowering the freezing point of water to prevent ice formation on roads—effective down to about -9°C (ΔTf = iKfm, i = 2 for NaCl)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Reaction Rates.
